title: Metástasis Cerebrales
subtitle: Block A — Neuro-Oncology: Surgical Resection, Radiosurgery, and Multimodal Management
guidelines_version: AANS/CNS Guidelines for Brain Metastases 2022 · ASTRO Brain Metastases Guidelines 2022 · NCCN CNS Tumors v1.2024
textbook_refs: Soffietti R et al. Diagnosis and treatment of brain metastases from solid tumours: guidelines from the European Association of Neuro-Oncology (EANO). Eur J Cancer 2017;75:105-115
Vogelbaum MA et al. Treatment for Brain Metastases: ASCO-SNO-ASTRO Guideline. J Clin Oncol 2022;40:492-516
Patchell RA et al. A randomized trial of surgery in the treatment of single metastases to the brain. N Engl J Med 1990;322:494-500
Kocher M et al. Adjuvant whole-brain radiotherapy versus observation after radiosurgery or surgical resection of 1-3 brain metastases. J Clin Oncol 2011;29:134-141
learning_objectives: Apply the RPA (Recursive Partitioning Analysis) and GPA (Graded Prognostic Assessment) classification systems to estimate prognosis and guide treatment selection
Define indications for surgical resection versus stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) for brain metastases based on size, number, location, and systemic disease status
Understand the role of adjuvant whole-brain radiotherapy (WBRT) and when it can be safely omitted in favour of active surveillance after local treatment
Describe the evidence base for resection cavity SRS after surgical removal of brain metastases
Select appropriate systemic therapy for brain metastases based on primary tumour histology and molecular profile (HER2, EGFR, ALK, BRAF, PIK3CA)
Identify clinical and radiological features that distinguish brain metastases from primary brain tumours

blocks.content:
Brain metastases are the most common intracranial malignancy in adults, occurring in 10-30% of cancer patients and outnumbering primary brain tumours by approximately 10:1. The incidence has been increasing due to improved survival of patients with systemic cancer and wider use of MRI surveillance. The most common primary tumours causing brain metastases are lung cancer (40-50%), breast cancer (15-25%), melanoma (5-20%), renal cell carcinoma (5-10%), and colorectal carcinoma (3-5%). Lung and melanoma metastases tend to be multiple; renal cell, colorectal, and breast tend to produce fewer lesions. **Melanoma** has the highest tendency for haemorrhage within metastases due to its high vascularity; other haemorrhagic metastases include renal cell carcinoma, choriocarcinoma, and thyroid carcinoma (the mnemonic: Me Rich Keeps Collecting CHocolate — Melanoma, Renal, Kaposi/Kidney, Choriocarcinoma, CHoroid plexus carcinoma, Thyroid).

The blood-brain barrier (BBB) limits the penetration of many chemotherapeutic agents, particularly large-molecule drugs, into brain metastases. However, metastatic lesions disrupt the BBB (as evidenced by gadolinium enhancement on MRI), and small-molecule targeted agents with lipophilicity and low molecular weight can achieve therapeutic CNS concentrations. Brain metastases are typically located at the grey-white junction (where the arterial tree narrows and acts as a micro-embolic trap), particularly in the watershed zone of the MCA territory. The cerebellum and brainstem receive 15-20% of lesions, proportional to their blood flow.

Diagnosis is primarily by **contrast-enhanced MRI brain** (gadolinium, 1.5 or 3T), with the standard protocol including pre- and post-contrast T1, T2/FLAIR, DWI, and GRE/SWI sequences. Thin-slice post-contrast T1 (1 mm isotropic) improves sensitivity for small metastases compared to standard 5 mm slices. MRI is superior to CT for detecting lesions in the posterior fossa and for identifying small (<1 cm) lesions. When MRI is equivocal, MR spectroscopy, perfusion imaging, or PET can help differentiate metastases from high-grade glioma or other lesions.

blocks.title: Most Common Primary Tumours Causing Brain Metastases
Board Pearl — Haemorrhagic Brain Metastases: The Classic Four
blocks.content: The four primary tumours most commonly causing haemorrhagic brain metastases are: Melanoma, Renal cell carcinoma, Choriocarcinoma, and Thyroid carcinoma. Lung and breast metastases can also haemorrhage but less characteristically. On CT/MRI, a haemorrhagic mass in the grey-white junction in a patient with known systemic malignancy should prompt consideration of these primaries.

sections.title: Prognostic Systems: RPA and GPA Classification

blocks.content:
Prognostic classification systems are essential for counselling patients and selecting appropriate treatment intensity, balancing survival benefit against quality of life and treatment burden. The **Recursive Partitioning Analysis (RPA)** classification (Gaspar et al. IJROBP 1997) was derived from RTOG WBRT trials and defines three classes based on KPS, primary tumour control, and extracranial metastases: Class I (KPS ≥70, age <65, controlled primary, no ECM) — median OS 7.1 months; Class II (all others not in I or III) — median OS 4.2 months; Class III (KPS <70) — median OS 2.3 months.

The **Graded Prognostic Assessment (GPA)** score (Sperduto et al. JCO 2010, updated 2012 with Diagnosis-Specific GPA, DS-GPA) improved upon RPA by incorporating tumour-specific molecular and clinical factors. The GPA assigns 0-1 points each for: age (0 pts if >60, 0.5 pts if 50-59, 1 pt if <50), KPS (0 pts if <70, 0.5 pts if 70-80, 1 pt if 90-100), number of brain metastases (0 pts if >3, 0.5 pts if 2-3, 1 pt if 1), and extracranial metastases (0 pts if present, 1 pt if absent). Total score 0-4; higher score = better prognosis.

Critically, the **Diagnosis-Specific GPA** incorporates tumour-specific factors with substantially different predictors by histology. For **NSCLC**, EGFR and ALK status are included — EGFR-mutated NSCLC with GPA 3.5-4.0 has median OS of 46.8 months (vs 6.9 months for GPA 0-1). For **breast cancer**, molecular subtype (HR+/HER2−, HER2+, triple-negative) is the dominant prognostic factor — HER2+ with GPA 3.5-4 achieves median OS 25.3 months. For **melanoma**, BRAF V600E status is incorporated — BRAF-mutated melanoma with GPA 3.5-4 achieves median OS 13.2 months. These tumour-specific prognostic systems inform the intensity of local treatment (surgery vs SRS vs WBRT) and guide integration of systemic targeted therapy.

blocks.title: Board Pearl — Diagnosis-Specific GPA Changes Prognosis Dramatically
blocks.content: The Diagnosis-Specific GPA (DS-GPA) shows that median OS varies 10-fold depending on histology and molecular subtype, even with the same GPA score. EGFR-mutated NSCLC with GPA 3.5-4: median OS 46.8 months. Versus KRAS-mutated NSCLC GPA 0-1: median OS 3.4 months. HER2+ breast cancer is the second most favourable subtype. This is why molecular profiling of the primary tumour is mandatory before deciding on aggressive local treatment for brain metastases.

The landmark **Patchell et al. (NEJM 1990)** randomised controlled trial established the benefit of surgical resection for a single brain metastasis. This trial randomised 48 patients with a single accessible brain metastasis to surgery + WBRT versus needle biopsy + WBRT. The surgery group had significantly longer median OS (40 vs 15 weeks), fewer deaths from neurological causes (14% vs 44%), longer functional independence (38 vs 8 weeks), and lower local recurrence rate (20% vs 52%). A second landmark RCT by **Vecht et al. (Ann Neurol 1993)** confirmed these findings: surgery + WBRT vs WBRT alone in single brain metastasis with systemic disease — surgery group: median OS 10 months vs 6 months (p=0.04). Importantly, the benefit was restricted to patients with stable or absent systemic disease (OS 12 vs 7 months), while patients with active systemic disease showed no benefit (OS 5 months in both groups) — establishing that systemic disease control is critical to the surgical benefit.

**Current indications for surgical resection of brain metastases:**
- Single or dominant symptomatic metastasis (>3 cm, significant mass effect, or neurological compromise)
- Lesion in accessible location and patient with good functional status (KPS ≥70) and controlled or controllable systemic disease
- Histological diagnosis needed (unknown primary, or atypical MRI features)
- Radiation-resistant tumours (RCC, melanoma) where SRS monotherapy may be insufficient for large lesions
- Posterior fossa metastasis with obstructive hydrocephalus requiring urgent decompression
- Prior WBRT precluding SRS due to cumulative dose

**Surgical technique**: the goal is safe gross total resection via a tailored craniotomy. Awake craniotomy with cortical mapping is used for lesions in or near eloquent cortex. **En-bloc** resection (preferred over piecemeal) reduces leptomeningeal dissemination risk — the **Patchell 1998** trial of resection cavity WBRT vs observation demonstrated higher local recurrence with observation (46% vs 10%), establishing that local adjuvant treatment is always required after resection.

blocks.title: Common Pitfall — Patchell 1990 Benefit Limited to Stable Systemic Disease
blocks.content: The Patchell 1990 surgical benefit (OS 40 vs 15 weeks) was specifically in patients with good KPS and limited systemic disease. The Vecht 1993 trial confirmed: surgery + WBRT benefits patients with controlled systemic disease (OS 12 vs 7 months) but NOT patients with active systemic disease (OS 5 months both groups). Do not recommend aggressive craniotomy for a patient with rapidly progressive systemic metastatic disease and poor performance status — palliation is the goal.

blocks.title: Clinical Context — Surgery vs SRS Decision
blocks.content: A 58-year-old woman, KPS 90, known breast cancer (HER2+), presents with a single 3.8 cm right frontoparietal brain metastasis causing mild left hemiparesis. Systemic disease controlled on pertuzumab/trastuzumab. GPA score 3.0 (DS-GPA for HER2+ breast): surgery is preferred over SRS alone for this size lesion with mass effect and neurological symptoms, providing immediate decompression and tissue for molecular analysis. Post-operative resection cavity SRS (rather than WBRT) preserves cognitive function.

sections.title: Stereotactic Radiosurgery: Indications, Dose, and Evidence

blocks.content:
Stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) has become the dominant local treatment modality for brain metastases over the past two decades, largely replacing whole-brain radiotherapy (WBRT) as first-line treatment for patients with limited numbers of metastases. SRS delivers a precisely targeted high dose of radiation in one to five fractions, achieving local control rates of 80-92% at 12 months for lesions ≤3 cm.

**Dose selection**: single-fraction SRS doses for brain metastases are determined by lesion volume, as defined in the RTOG 90-05 dose-escalation study: lesions ≤20 mm diameter → 24 Gy; 21-30 mm → 18 Gy; 31-40 mm → 15 Gy. Lesions >40 mm are generally not treated with single-fraction SRS due to unacceptable radionecrosis risk. Fractionated SRS (FSRS, 3-5 fractions at 7-9 Gy per fraction) is preferred for lesions >2 cm, those adjacent to eloquent structures, or previously irradiated lesions. The biologically effective dose (BED) of fractionated SRS is equivalent or superior to single-fraction for local control while reducing radionecrosis risk.

**SRS vs WBRT: the definitive evidence.** The **EORTC 22952-26001 trial** (Kocher et al. JCO 2011) randomised 359 patients with 1-3 brain metastases who underwent either surgery or SRS, then randomised to WBRT vs observation. WBRT reduced 2-year intracranial relapse (48% vs 78% without WBRT) but did NOT improve overall survival (10.7 months WBRT vs 10.9 months without). WBRT significantly impaired neurocognitive function at 4 months (Hopkins Verbal Learning Test: 52% WBRT decline vs 24% observation). This landmark trial established that omitting WBRT after local treatment does not harm survival and preserves neurocognition.

The **NCCTG N0574 trial** (Brown et al. JAMA 2016) further confirmed: SRS alone vs SRS + WBRT in 1-3 brain metastases — no OS difference; WBRT arm had significantly worse cognitive decline at 3 months (92% decline vs 64%). The **MD Anderson SRS alone vs WBRT** data (Chang et al. Lancet Oncol 2009) demonstrated the same — WBRT causes clinically significant memory decline measured by HVLT-R.

**SRS for ≥4 metastases**: Multiple prospective studies and meta-analyses demonstrate that SRS to 4-10 (or more) metastases achieves OS and local control equivalent to WBRT, provided total intracranial volume is ≤10 cm³. The **Japanese phase III trial** (Aoyama et al. JAMA 2006) of SRS ± WBRT in 1-4 metastases showed no OS difference. Contemporary practice at most major centres offers SRS to patients with up to 10-15 metastases when total volume is manageable.

blocks.title: RTOG 90-05 Radiosurgery Dose Recommendations by Lesion Size
rows: Not recommended SRS alone; consider fractionated or surgery

sections.title: Whole-Brain Radiotherapy: Role and Cognitive Considerations

blocks.content:
Whole-brain radiotherapy (WBRT) was the historical standard treatment for brain metastases and remains important in specific clinical scenarios. WBRT delivers 30 Gy in 10 fractions (3 Gy/fraction) or 20 Gy in 5 fractions (palliative) to the entire brain parenchyma, treating both visible metastases and presumed microscopic disease. While it reduces intracranial relapse (both local and distant), multiple level I evidence trials (EORTC 22952, NCCTG N0574, MD Anderson) have demonstrated NO overall survival benefit from adding WBRT to SRS for patients with 1-3 metastases.

Current **indications for WBRT** (where it remains the appropriate choice):
- Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis (in combination with intrathecal therapy or as palliation)
- Extensive brain metastases (>15 lesions or total volume >15 cm³) not amenable to SRS
- Small cell lung cancer (prophylactic cranial irradiation, PCI, and therapeutic WBRT)
- Patient with poor performance status (KPS <70) where WBRT provides simple, efficient palliation
- Failure of multiple SRS courses with widespread intracranial progression
- Radiosensitive histologies (SCLC, lymphoma, germ cell) where WBRT is highly effective

**Hippocampal avoidance WBRT (HA-WBRT)** with memantine was tested in **RTOG 0933** (Gondi et al. JCO 2014, phase II) and the randomised **NRG CC001 trial** (Brown et al. JCO 2020). NRG CC001 demonstrated that HA-WBRT + memantine vs standard WBRT + memantine significantly reduced cognitive failure at 6 months (hazard ratio for cognitive failure 0.74, p=0.02) while maintaining equivalent intracranial control. HA-WBRT should be standard practice when WBRT is given, sparing the hippocampus to ≤9 Gy (V100% constraint).

**Memantine** (NMDA receptor antagonist, 5-20 mg/day, titrated over 3 weeks) is co-administered with WBRT based on phase III evidence from **RTOG 0614** (Brown et al. Neuro Oncol 2013), which demonstrated delayed time to cognitive decline with memantine vs placebo, particularly memory function at 24 weeks.

blocks.title: Board Pearl — WBRT Does Not Improve OS in 1-3 Brain Metastases
blocks.content: Key message from EORTC 22952-26001 (Kocher JCO 2011) and NCCTG N0574 (Brown JAMA 2016): adding WBRT to SRS or surgery for 1-3 brain metastases does NOT improve overall survival (approximately 10-11 months in both arms). WBRT DOES reduce intracranial relapse (48% vs 78% at 2 years) but at the cost of significant neurocognitive decline (52% HVLT-R decline vs 24%). Therefore, omit WBRT after local treatment for patients with 1-3 (or more) metastases; offer close MRI surveillance instead.

sections.title: Resection Cavity SRS and Leptomeningeal Dissemination

blocks.content:
After surgical resection of a brain metastasis, the resection cavity requires local adjuvant treatment to prevent local recurrence. The choice between WBRT and resection cavity SRS (also termed 'surgical cavity SRS' or 'post-operative SRS') has been definitively addressed by the **Alliance A071801 trial** (Brown et al. JAMA Oncol 2022) and **NCCTG N107C/CEC3** (Brown et al. Lancet Oncol 2017). The NCCTG N107C trial randomised 194 patients after resection of a single brain metastasis to SRS of the resection cavity vs WBRT. Results: no significant difference in OS (12.2 months SRS vs 11.6 months WBRT) or seizure control, but SRS preserved cognition significantly better at 6 months (cognitive failure: 52.4% SRS vs 85.4% WBRT, p=0.00031) with equivalent 12-month local failure rates (21.1% SRS vs 19.3% WBRT).

**Resection cavity SRS technique considerations**: The timing of SRS after surgery is critical. Early SRS (≤4 weeks post-op) is associated with higher radionecrosis rates due to surgical inflammation. The recommended timing is 4-6 weeks post-operatively. The target volume includes the resection cavity with a 1-2 mm margin, avoiding excessive normal brain inclusion. Typical doses: 15-20 Gy single fraction for cavities ≤3 cm, 21-24 Gy for smaller cavities; fractionated SRS (25-27 Gy in 3 fractions or 30 Gy in 5 fractions) preferred for larger cavities (>3 cm) to reduce radionecrosis.

**Leptomeningeal dissemination (LMD)** after brain metastasis resection is a feared complication, occurring in approximately 10-14% of patients following piecemeal resection vs 5-6% after en-bloc resection. The **TUMOR SEEDING** risk is particularly relevant for posterior fossa (cerebellar) metastases, where piecemeal removal increases LMD risk due to proximity of CSF pathways. En-bloc resection technique, avoidance of tumour entry during dissection, and copious irrigation are the surgical strategies to minimise LMD. Post-operative contrast MRI within 48 hours documents baseline resection status and serves as the template for cavity SRS planning.

blocks.title: Common Pitfall — Piecemeal Resection Increases Leptomeningeal Dissemination
blocks.content: Piecemeal (internally debulking before capsule dissection) resection of cerebellar metastases is associated with leptomeningeal carcinomatosis in up to 14% of cases. En-bloc resection with intact tumour capsule dramatically reduces this risk. For cerebellar and posterior fossa metastases, maintain the arachnoidal plane and avoid entering the tumour until the capsule is fully dissected free. This is especially critical for melanoma and lung adenocarcinoma metastases which have high LMD propensity.

sections.title: Systemic Therapy with CNS Penetration and Molecular Targets

blocks.content:
Advances in molecularly targeted therapy have transformed the management of brain metastases, with several agents demonstrating meaningful intracranial activity due to their CNS-penetrant properties. The concept of 'intracranial response rate' (iORR) is now a standard endpoint in trials of agents for CNS disease.

**NSCLC with EGFR mutations**: Third-generation EGFR TKI **osimertinib** (AZD9291) achieves 70-91% intracranial response rates in EGFR-mutated NSCLC brain metastases. The **FLAURA trial** (Soria et al. NEJM 2018) demonstrated osimertinib superiority over first-generation TKIs in EGFR-mutated NSCLC; the CNS subgroup (91 patients with brain mets) showed CNS PFS HR 0.48, intracranial ORR 66% vs 43%. The brain-penetrant property of osimertinib (CNS:plasma ratio ~40%) is a key advantage for CNS disease management. Many centres defer SRS for small asymptomatic EGFR-mutated NSCLC brain metastases in favour of osimertinib alone with close surveillance.

**NSCLC with ALK rearrangements**: **Alectinib** and **lorlatinib** (3rd-generation ALK inhibitor) achieve high intracranial control. The **J-ALEX trial** (Hida et al. Lancet 2017) and **ALEX trial** (Peters et al. NEJM 2017) showed alectinib's superior intracranial activity over crizotinib. Lorlatinib has demonstrated activity in CNS-progressive disease after prior ALK inhibitors.

**Melanoma with BRAF V600E mutation**: **Dabrafenib + trametinib** (BRAF/MEK inhibition) achieves 50-58% intracranial response in BRAF V600E-mutated melanoma brain metastases (COMBI-MB trial, Davies et al. Lancet Oncol 2017). Immunotherapy with **ipilimumab + nivolumab** (CheckMate 204, ABC trial) achieves 57-82% intracranial responses in melanoma brain metastases depending on corticosteroid use.

**HER2+ breast cancer**: **Tucatinib + trastuzumab + capecitabine** (HER2CLIMB trial, Murthy et al. NEJM 2020) demonstrated significantly improved PFS in HER2+ breast cancer with brain metastases (HR 0.48, median PFS 7.6 vs 5.4 months) and reduced CNS progression rate. **T-DXd** (trastuzumab deruxtecan, DESTINY-Breast06) also showing intracranial activity.

**Corticosteroids** (dexamethasone, typically 4-8 mg twice daily) reduce peritumoral oedema and are used to provide acute symptom relief in patients with symptomatic brain metastases. However, dexamethasone impairs immune function and reduces efficacy of checkpoint inhibitors — steroids should be tapered to the lowest effective dose or discontinued before initiating immunotherapy when oncologically appropriate.

blocks.title: CNS-Active Targeted Agents by Primary Tumour Histology
Clinical Context — Deferring SRS for EGFR-Mutated NSCLC Brain Metastases
blocks.content: A 55-year-old non-smoker with EGFR exon 19 deletion NSCLC presents with 3 asymptomatic brain metastases ≤1.5 cm each and no extracranial disease progression on osimertinib. Intracranial disease may be managed with osimertinib continuation and close MRI surveillance (every 3 months) before committing to SRS. When SRS is ultimately needed, osimertinib should be held for ≥2 days around SRS to reduce radiation sensitisation risk. This approach — deferring local therapy in EGFR-mutated patients on osimertinib — is supported by retrospective data but remains an area of ongoing clinical investigation.

consolidation.intro: Test your knowledge of brain metastasis management with 20 board-style questions.

questions.stem: Which primary tumour has the HIGHEST propensity for haemorrhage within brain metastases?

explanation.correct: Melanoma has the highest propensity for intratumoral haemorrhage among brain metastases due to its highly vascular nature, tendency to form abnormal tumour vessels, and intrinsic melanin content that predisposes to bleeding. Up to 40-50% of melanoma brain metastases show haemorrhage on MRI (GRE/SWI sequences). Other primary tumours with high haemorrhagic risk include renal cell carcinoma, choriocarcinoma, and thyroid carcinoma. NSCLC is the most common cause of brain metastases overall but has a relatively low haemorrhage rate compared to melanoma.

wrong.A: NSCLC is the most common primary tumour causing brain metastases but has a relatively low haemorrhagic tendency compared to melanoma, RCC, and choriocarcinoma.
wrong.C: Colorectal carcinoma brain metastases are uncommon and have a low haemorrhage risk. They tend to be solitary and posterior fossa-located in some series.
wrong.D: Prostate carcinoma rarely metastasises to brain parenchyma (it preferentially involves bone and leptomeninges/dura). Brain parenchymal metastases from prostate cancer are uncommon and not particularly haemorrhagic.

explanation.guideline: AANS/CNS Guidelines for Brain Metastases 2022 — Diagnosis section; NCCN CNS Tumors v1.2024
explanation.trial: Nussbaum ES et al. J Neurosurg 1996: Haemorrhagic brain metastases series — melanoma 39.9% haemorrhage rate vs 5.4% for NSCLC
explanation.pearl: Haemorrhagic brain metastases: Melanoma > Renal cell > Choriocarcinoma > Thyroid. Melanoma is the classic answer. On SWI/GRE, multiple haemorrhagic lesions in an oncology patient = think melanoma or RCC first.

questions.stem: The Patchell 1990 trial established the benefit of surgical resection for single brain metastases. In which patient subgroup was the survival benefit of surgery MOST clearly demonstrated by the subsequent Vecht 1993 trial?

options.A: Patients with actively progressive systemic metastatic disease
options.B: Patients with KPS <70 regardless of systemic disease status
options.C: Patients with stable or absent extracranial disease

explanation.correct: The Vecht et al. (Ann Neurol 1993) trial specifically examined surgery + WBRT vs WBRT alone stratified by systemic disease activity. In patients with stable or absent extracranial disease, surgery significantly improved OS (12 vs 7 months, p=0.04). Crucially, in patients with active systemic disease, there was NO survival benefit from surgery (OS 5 months in both groups). This finding established that controlled systemic disease is a prerequisite for deriving meaningful survival benefit from surgical resection of brain metastases.

wrong.A: Patients with actively progressive systemic disease did NOT benefit from surgery in the Vecht 1993 trial — OS was identical (5 months) in both arms. Surgery should not be recommended for patients with uncontrolled systemic disease.
wrong.B: KPS <70 is a negative prognostic factor (RPA class III, median OS 2.3 months) and surgery in this group is generally not beneficial. The surgical benefit was seen in KPS ≥70 patients.
wrong.D: Posterior fossa location was not specifically identified as the subgroup with greatest surgical benefit in either Patchell or Vecht. Posterior fossa metastases may require surgery for obstructive hydrocephalus regardless of systemic status.

explanation.guideline: AANS/CNS Guidelines for Brain Metastases 2022 — Surgery section: Grade A recommendation for surgery in single brain metastasis with controlled systemic disease and good KPS
explanation.trial: Vecht CJ et al. Ann Neurol 1993: 63 patients, single brain met — surgery + WBRT vs WBRT alone; benefit in stable extracranial disease (OS 12 vs 7 months) but NOT in active systemic disease (OS 5 vs 5 months)
explanation.pearl: Vecht 1993: surgery for single brain met only benefits patients with CONTROLLED systemic disease. Active systemic progression = no survival benefit from surgery. This is the key subgroup analysis that defines surgical indication.

questions.stem: A patient has 2 brain metastases (1.2 cm and 0.8 cm), KPS 80, age 55, no extracranial metastases, from NSCLC. What is their GPA score?

explanation.correct: GPA scoring: Age 55 (50-59) = 0.5 pts; KPS 80 = 0.5 pts; Number of brain mets = 2 (2-3) = 0.5 pts; Extracranial metastases = absent = 1.0 pt. Total = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 + 1.0 = 2.5 pts. Wait — recalculating: Age 55 → 0.5; KPS 80 → 0.5; 2 brain mets → 0.5; No extracranial mets → 1.0. Total GPA = 2.5. However, for NSCLC Diagnosis-Specific GPA, molecular status (EGFR, ALK) would modify this score. The straightforward GPA calculation gives 2.5.

wrong.A: 1.5 would require age >60 (0 pts) + KPS 70-80 (0.5) + 2-3 mets (0.5) + absent ECM (1.0) = 2.0, or some other low-scoring combination. This score underestimates this patient's GPA.
wrong.B: 2.5 is actually the correct GPA score for this patient. But given the answer options, 3.0 represents an error in calculation — let me re-examine: Age 55 = 0.5; KPS 80 = 0.5; 2 brain mets = 0.5; No ECM = 1.0. Sum = 2.5. Answer B (2.5) is correct.
wrong.D: 3.5 would require near-perfect scores across all categories (e.g., age <50 = 1.0; KPS 90-100 = 1.0; 1 met = 1.0; no ECM = 1.0 = 4.0, or 3 factors maximal + 1 intermediate = 3.5). This patient scores 2.5.

explanation.guideline: Sperduto PW et al. JCO 2010: Graded Prognostic Assessment (GPA) for brain metastases — scoring system and median OS by score range
explanation.trial: Sperduto PW et al. J Clin Oncol 2012: Diagnosis-specific GPA (DS-GPA) — NSCLC-specific GPA incorporating EGFR/ALK status; GPA 2.5-3.0 associated with median OS 11-15 months for NSCLC
explanation.pearl: GPA = sum of 4 components (age, KPS, number of mets, extracranial mets), each 0-1.0 pts; total 0-4.0. Higher score = better prognosis. For NSCLC, DS-GPA adds EGFR and ALK status — EGFR+ GPA 3.5-4 achieves median OS 46.8 months.

questions.stem: The NCCTG N107C trial (Brown et al. Lancet Oncol 2017) compared resection cavity SRS versus WBRT after surgical resection of a single brain metastasis. What was the primary finding regarding cognition?

options.A: WBRT preserved cognition significantly better than SRS at 6 months
options.B: No difference in cognitive function was observed between the two arms
options.C: SRS significantly preserved cognitive function compared to WBRT (cognitive failure 52% SRS vs 85% WBRT at 6 months)
options.D: SRS caused higher rates of radionecrosis, which offset the cognitive benefit

explanation.correct: The NCCTG N107C/CEC3 trial (Brown et al. Lancet Oncol 2017) randomised 194 patients after resection of single brain metastasis to post-operative cavity SRS vs WBRT. The primary finding was that SRS significantly preserved cognitive function: cognitive decline at 6 months occurred in 52.4% of SRS patients vs 85.4% of WBRT patients (p=0.00031). There was no significant difference in overall survival (12.2 vs 11.6 months) or 12-month intracranial control rates (21.1% SRS vs 19.3% WBRT). This established resection cavity SRS as the preferred adjuvant strategy after brain metastasis resection, replacing WBRT in eligible patients.

wrong.A: The opposite was found — WBRT caused significantly more cognitive decline than SRS. WBRT cognitive failure was 85.4% vs 52.4% for SRS at 6 months.
wrong.B: A highly significant difference in cognitive function was demonstrated — the p-value of 0.00031 indicates an overwhelming difference, not equivalence.
wrong.D: While radionecrosis rates may be slightly higher with SRS vs WBRT, this did not offset the substantial cognitive benefit. The N107C trial did not show a clinically significant increase in radionecrosis that affected the overall benefit of SRS over WBRT.

explanation.guideline: AANS/CNS Guidelines for Brain Metastases 2022 — Post-surgical adjuvant: resection cavity SRS preferred over WBRT based on N107C trial; ASTRO Brain Metastases Guidelines 2022
explanation.trial: Brown PD et al. Lancet Oncol 2017 (NCCTG N107C/CEC3): 194 pts after single brain met resection — SRS cavity vs WBRT; 6-month cognitive failure 52.4% SRS vs 85.4% WBRT (p=0.00031); OS 12.2 vs 11.6 months (NS)
explanation.pearl: N107C/CEC3: resection cavity SRS vs WBRT after single brain met resection — equivalent OS and local control, but dramatically better cognitive preservation with SRS (52% vs 85% cognitive failure). Cavity SRS is now standard, not WBRT, after brain met resection.

questions.stem: Per RTOG 90-05 dose-escalation trial, what is the recommended single-fraction SRS dose for a brain metastasis measuring 25 mm in maximum diameter?

explanation.correct: RTOG 90-05 established dose-response and tolerance data for single-fraction SRS in recurrent brain tumours. The recommended doses by size are: ≤20 mm → 24 Gy; 21-30 mm → 18 Gy; 31-40 mm → 15 Gy. A 25 mm lesion falls in the 21-30 mm range, corresponding to 18 Gy. These doses were selected to balance local control against radionecrosis risk, with higher doses for smaller lesions exploiting the steep dose-response relationship and the cubic relationship between volume and normal brain exposure.

wrong.A: 24 Gy is the recommended dose for lesions ≤20 mm maximum diameter, not for 25 mm. Applying 24 Gy to a 25 mm lesion carries higher radionecrosis risk without demonstrated additional control benefit.
wrong.C: 15 Gy is the recommended dose for lesions 31-40 mm. The 25 mm lesion is in the intermediate size range (21-30 mm) where 18 Gy is appropriate.
wrong.D: 12 Gy is used for certain specific indications (e.g., optic nerve sheath, re-irradiation, cavernous sinus meningiomas) but is not the RTOG 90-05 recommended dose for a 25 mm brain metastasis. This dose would be subtherapeutic for a metastasis.

explanation.guideline: RTOG 90-05 (Shaw E et al. IJROBP 2000): Phase I/II dose-escalation SRS trial establishing current dose-size recommendations; ASTRO Brain Metastases Guidelines 2022 — SRS dosing
explanation.trial: Shaw E et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2000 (RTOG 90-05): 156 patients, brain tumours ≤40 mm, dose escalation — recommended doses: ≤20 mm = 24 Gy; 21-30 mm = 18 Gy; 31-40 mm = 15 Gy; these doses remain standard 25 years later
explanation.pearl: RTOG 90-05 SRS dose by size: ≤20 mm = 24 Gy; 21-30 mm = 18 Gy; 31-40 mm = 15 Gy. Memorise these three thresholds — they appear frequently in board questions about radiosurgery for brain metastases.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with HER2-amplified breast cancer develops 2 new brain metastases (both 1.2 cm) while receiving pertuzumab + trastuzumab. Systemic disease is controlled. What is the most appropriate systemic therapy change for the CNS component?

options.A: Initiate bevacizumab 10 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks for CNS disease
options.B: Switch to tucatinib + trastuzumab + capecitabine (HER2CLIMB regimen)
options.C: Initiate temozolomide 150-200 mg/m² days 1-5 every 28 days
options.D: Continue current regimen — pertuzumab/trastuzumab is sufficient for CNS disease

explanation.correct: The HER2CLIMB trial (Murthy et al. NEJM 2020) demonstrated that tucatinib + trastuzumab + capecitabine significantly improved outcomes specifically in patients with HER2+ breast cancer brain metastases. In the brain metastasis subgroup: CNS PFS HR 0.48 (p<0.001), 1-year CNS PFS 40% vs 0% with placebo arm, intracranial ORR 47.3% vs 20%. Tucatinib has demonstrated CNS penetration. For HER2+ breast cancer progressing in the CNS on pertuzumab/trastuzumab, the HER2CLIMB regimen is the evidence-based systemic therapy change. Local treatment (SRS) should also be considered for the two 1.2 cm lesions.

wrong.A: Bevacizumab reduces peritumoral oedema (via anti-VEGF mechanism) and has some activity against brain metastases radionecrosis but is not a standard CNS-active systemic therapy for HER2+ breast cancer brain metastases. It does not have the targeted HER2 activity needed.
wrong.C: Temozolomide has no established activity in breast cancer brain metastases. It is used for GBM and some other primary brain tumours but not for metastatic breast cancer.
wrong.D: Continuing pertuzumab + trastuzumab alone is insufficient — this regimen does not provide adequate CNS penetration and the patient has demonstrated CNS progression on this therapy. A CNS-active regimen change is indicated.

explanation.guideline: ASCO-SNO-ASTRO Brain Metastases Guideline 2022 — HER2+ breast cancer CNS metastases: tucatinib-based regimen preferred for CNS progression; NCCN Breast Cancer v2024
explanation.trial: Murthy RK et al. N Engl J Med 2020 (HER2CLIMB): 612 pts HER2+ breast cancer (with or without brain mets) — tucatinib+T+Cape vs placebo+T+Cape; brain met subgroup: CNS PFS HR 0.48, p<0.001; 1-yr CNS PFS 40% vs 0%
explanation.pearl: HER2+ breast cancer brain mets progressing on pertuzumab/trastuzumab: switch to HER2CLIMB regimen (tucatinib + trastuzumab + capecitabine). HER2CLIMB trial showed CNS PFS HR 0.48 — this is the definitive CNS-active regimen for HER2+ breast cancer with brain involvement.

questions.stem: The EORTC 22952-26001 trial (Kocher et al. JCO 2011) randomised patients with 1-3 brain metastases after surgery or SRS to WBRT vs observation. What was the primary finding regarding overall survival?

options.A: WBRT significantly improved overall survival (10.9 vs 10.7 months, p=0.89)
options.B: Observation significantly improved overall survival by avoiding WBRT toxicity
options.C: WBRT significantly improved overall survival for patients with 2-3 metastases only
options.D: WBRT did not improve overall survival but reduced intracranial relapse at the cost of neurocognitive decline

explanation.correct: EORTC 22952-26001 (Kocher et al. JCO 2011) is the landmark RCT (n=359) comparing adjuvant WBRT vs observation after SRS or surgery for 1-3 brain metastases. WBRT reduced 2-year intracranial relapse at initial treatment sites (19% vs 31%) and at new brain sites (29% vs 47%), but there was NO difference in overall survival (10.7 months WBRT vs 10.9 months observation, HR 0.98, p=0.89). WBRT was associated with significantly worse performance status at 8 weeks and measurable neurocognitive decline. This trial established that omitting WBRT after local treatment is safe with respect to survival.

wrong.A: The survival figures are correct (10.7 vs 10.9 months, p=0.89) but this answer states WBRT 'significantly improved' survival when in fact there was NO significant difference — the result shows WBRT did NOT improve survival.
wrong.B: Observation did not 'improve' OS — both arms had equivalent OS. The advantage of observation was the avoidance of WBRT-associated neurocognitive toxicity, not an OS benefit.
wrong.C: The 2-3 metastasis subgroup analysis in EORTC 22952 did not show a survival benefit for WBRT in this specific subgroup. All subgroups showed equivalent OS with WBRT and observation.

explanation.guideline: AANS/CNS Guidelines for Brain Metastases 2022 — WBRT omission: Grade A recommendation to omit WBRT after local treatment for 1-3 metastases based on EORTC 22952 and NCCTG N0574
explanation.trial: Kocher M et al. J Clin Oncol 2011 (EORTC 22952-26001): 359 pts, 1-3 brain mets after SRS or surgery — WBRT vs observation; OS 10.7 vs 10.9 months (HR 0.98, p=0.89); 2-yr intracranial relapse 48% WBRT vs 78% obs (p<0.001); WBRT worsened performance status
explanation.pearl: EORTC 22952: WBRT after local treatment for 1-3 brain mets reduces intracranial relapse but does NOT improve OS. WBRT causes neurocognitive harm. Standard is: omit WBRT, perform SRS/surgery + close MRI surveillance (every 3 months). Salvage SRS for new lesions is equivalent in OS.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with NSCLC (EGFR exon 19 deletion, on osimertinib for 18 months) develops 4 new brain metastases (all <1.5 cm) without extracranial progression. He is asymptomatic. What is the most appropriate management of the CNS disease?

options.B: Switch EGFR TKI to gefitinib as osimertinib has failed
options.C: SRS to all 4 brain metastases; continue osimertinib
options.D: Brain biopsy of one lesion before any treatment to confirm metastatic histology

explanation.correct: In EGFR-mutated NSCLC on osimertinib with isolated CNS progression (no extracranial progression), local CNS therapy with SRS to all visible metastases combined with continuation of osimertinib is the standard approach. CNS-only progression on osimertinib may represent pharmacokinetic sanctuary effect rather than true resistance. SRS achieves high intracranial control, and continuing osimertinib controls the extracranial disease. WBRT is not indicated for 4 small asymptomatic metastases in an EGFR-mutated patient on an active systemic agent with excellent CNS penetration.

wrong.A: WBRT is not first-line for 4 small asymptomatic brain metastases in an otherwise well patient on an active CNS-penetrant TKI. WBRT causes cognitive decline without OS benefit vs SRS and should be deferred.
wrong.B: Switching to gefitinib would be a step backward — gefitinib is a first-generation EGFR TKI with inferior CNS penetration and activity compared to osimertinib. Osimertinib remains the standard TKI for EGFR-mutated NSCLC, including in CNS progression.
wrong.D: Brain biopsy is not indicated when CNS metastases are radiologically classic (grey-white junction, rounded, enhancement, known primary), multiple, and occur in the context of known active NSCLC with established EGFR mutation. The risk of biopsy does not justify tissue confirmation in this clinical context.

explanation.guideline: ASCO-SNO-ASTRO Brain Metastases Guideline 2022 — EGFR-mutated NSCLC with isolated CNS progression on osimertinib: local CNS therapy + continue osimertinib; NCCN NSCLC v2024
explanation.trial: Wu YL et al. Lancet Oncol 2018 (AURA3 CNS subgroup): Osimertinib superior to platinum-chemotherapy for intracranial ORR (70% vs 31%) in EGFR-mutated NSCLC T790M after first TKI; FLAURA CNS subgroup: osimertinib CNS PFS HR 0.48
explanation.pearl: EGFR-mutated NSCLC, isolated CNS progression on osimertinib: SRS to visible metastases + continue osimertinib. CNS-only progression ≠ systemic failure. Osimertinib's CNS:plasma ratio ~40% makes it the most CNS-penetrant EGFR TKI. Switching TKI is incorrect.

questions.stem: According to the Patchell 1998 trial, what was the effect of post-operative WBRT on local recurrence at the resection site after surgical removal of a single brain metastasis?

options.A: WBRT reduced local recurrence from 46% to 10%
options.B: WBRT had no significant effect on local recurrence
options.C: WBRT increased local recurrence due to radio-necrosis masking true recurrence
options.D: WBRT reduced distant brain recurrence but had no effect on the resection site

explanation.correct: Patchell et al. (JAMA 1998) randomised 95 patients after complete resection of single brain metastasis to WBRT vs observation. WBRT dramatically reduced local recurrence at the resection site (10% vs 46%, p<0.001) and distant brain recurrence (14% vs 37%, p=0.01). Despite these large differences in intracranial control, there was NO difference in overall survival or duration of functional independence. This established the paradox that WBRT controls intracranial disease but does not improve OS — later resolved by N107C showing cavity SRS can achieve equivalent local control to WBRT without cognitive harm.

wrong.A: This is the correct answer describing the Patchell 1998 finding exactly.
wrong.B: WBRT had a highly significant effect on local recurrence, reducing it from 46% to 10%. The lack of OS benefit despite improved local control is what made the result surprising and impactful.
wrong.C: WBRT reduced (not increased) local recurrence. Radionecrosis can mimic recurrence on MRI but this is a complication of treatment, not a cause of increased true recurrence.
wrong.D: WBRT reduced BOTH local recurrence at the resection site (10% vs 46%) AND distant brain recurrence (14% vs 37%). It was not limited to distant brain control.

explanation.guideline: AANS/CNS Guidelines for Brain Metastases 2022 — Historical role of WBRT post-resection superseded by cavity SRS (N107C trial)
explanation.trial: Patchell RA et al. JAMA 1998: 95 pts after single brain met resection — WBRT vs observation; local recurrence 10% WBRT vs 46% obs (p<0.001); distant brain 14% vs 37% (p=0.01); OS: no difference (11.1 vs 7.5 months, p=0.39)
explanation.pearl: Patchell 1998: WBRT after resection reduces local (10% vs 46%) and distant (14% vs 37%) recurrence but does NOT improve OS. This trial established the local treatment requirement after resection; N107C later showed cavity SRS achieves equivalent control with less cognitive toxicity.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute onset left hemiplegia and headache. He has no known malignancy. CT brain shows a 3.5 cm right frontoparietal mass with heterogeneous enhancement, surrounding oedema, and haemorrhage, at the grey-white junction. No other lesions. What is the most important immediate diagnostic consideration?

options.C: Metastatic lesion vs high-grade glioma — urgent MRI brain + systemic staging

explanation.correct: A 3.5 cm mass at the grey-white junction with heterogeneous enhancement, surrounding oedema, and haemorrhage in a patient without prior neurological history is most likely either a brain metastasis (unknown primary) or a high-grade glioma. The grey-white junction location strongly favours metastasis (the site of arterial calibre reduction trapping micro-emboli). Immediate management: urgent contrast MRI brain for better characterisation, dexamethasone 10 mg loading dose then 4 mg qid for oedema, and systemic staging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, PET-CT) to identify a primary tumour before neurosurgical intervention. Primary tumour identification changes management (biopsy sufficient vs GTR, systemic therapy selection).

wrong.A: Thrombolysis is absolutely contraindicated when there is haemorrhage within the mass. Additionally, the presentation (enhancing mass with oedema) is not characteristic of acute ischaemic stroke. Thrombolysis in this scenario would be catastrophic.
wrong.B: Cerebral abscess can mimic metastasis but typically shows restricted diffusion centrally on DWI and has a ring-enhancing pattern without the heterogeneous solid component described. More importantly, empirical antibiotics without tissue diagnosis does not address the primary mass.
wrong.D: Meningiomas are durally-based, not at the grey-white junction, and are not typically haemorrhagic. A meningioma does not fit the described imaging findings. More importantly, this is an emergency requiring urgent workup, not elective surgical planning.

explanation.guideline: AANS/CNS Guidelines for Brain Metastases 2022 — Diagnostic approach: contrast MRI + systemic staging mandatory before surgery; NCCN CNS Tumors v1.2024
explanation.trial: Barnholtz-Sloan JS et al. J Clin Oncol 2004: Population-based brain metastasis incidence study — 10-30% of cancer patients develop brain mets; grey-white junction location is pathognomonic feature of embolic metastasis
explanation.pearl: New brain mass at grey-white junction: brain metastasis until proven otherwise. Diagnostic steps: (1) contrast MRI brain, (2) CT chest/abdomen/pelvis for primary source, (3) dexamethasone for oedema. Grey-white junction + ring enhancement + multiple lesions = metastases. Single lesion may require surgery for diagnosis if primary unknown.

questions.stem: According to NRG CC001 (Brown et al. JCO 2020), hippocampal avoidance WBRT (HA-WBRT) with memantine compared to standard WBRT with memantine significantly reduced which outcome?

explanation.correct: NRG CC001 (Brown et al. JCO 2020) was a phase III RCT comparing HA-WBRT + memantine vs standard WBRT + memantine in patients with brain metastases. The primary endpoint was time to cognitive failure. HA-WBRT significantly reduced cognitive failure (HR 0.74, p=0.02), specifically delayed memory recall (HVLT-R delayed recall at 6 months: decline 11.5% HA-WBRT vs 24.7% standard WBRT). There was no difference in intracranial control or overall survival. This trial established HA-WBRT as the standard technique when WBRT is indicated, provided no tumours are within 5 mm of the hippocampus.

wrong.A: Intracranial tumour recurrence at 1 year was equivalent between HA-WBRT and standard WBRT, confirming that hippocampal sparing does not compromise tumour control (as metastases rarely occur in the hippocampus itself).
wrong.B: Neurological death and overall survival were equivalent between the two arms. The benefit was specifically in cognitive function preservation.
wrong.D: Grade 3-5 radiation toxicity was similar between the two arms. The benefit of HA-WBRT is specifically neurocognitive preservation, not reduction of acute radiation toxicity.

explanation.guideline: ASTRO Brain Metastases Guidelines 2022 — Recommends HA-WBRT with memantine when WBRT is indicated and no metastases within 5 mm of hippocampus; NRG CC001 is the evidence base
explanation.trial: Brown PD et al. J Clin Oncol 2020 (NRG CC001): 518 pts, WBRT + memantine vs HA-WBRT + memantine; cognitive failure HR 0.74, p=0.02; HVLT-R delayed recall 11.5% vs 24.7% decline; OS equivalent
explanation.pearl: NRG CC001: HA-WBRT + memantine vs standard WBRT + memantine — HA-WBRT reduces cognitive failure (HR 0.74). HA-WBRT constraint: hippocampus V100% ≤9 Gy. If metastases within 5 mm of hippocampus, HA technique cannot be safely applied.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old woman with BRAF V600E-mutated melanoma has 3 brain metastases (0.8 cm, 1.2 cm, 1.5 cm). She has extensive extracranial disease. ECOG PS 1. Which systemic therapy achieves the highest intracranial objective response rate specifically in BRAF V600E-mutated melanoma brain metastases?

explanation.correct: COMBI-MB trial (Davies et al. Lancet Oncol 2017) evaluated dabrafenib + trametinib in 4 cohorts of melanoma brain metastases stratified by BRAF V600E/V600K status and prior local brain therapy. In the largest cohort (V600E, no prior local brain therapy, ECOG 0-1): intracranial ORR 58%, intracranial complete response 22%, median intracranial PFS 5.6 months. This intracranial ORR (58%) exceeds that of ipilimumab + nivolumab in some comparisons, though the ABC trial and CheckMate 204 show comparable iORR (57-82%) for combination immunotherapy in asymptomatic patients. For BRAF V600E-mutated melanoma, the BRAF/MEK combination achieves the highest and most rapid intracranial responses.

wrong.A: Ipilimumab monotherapy achieves modest intracranial response rates in melanoma brain metastases (~12-18% iORR in historical series). The combination of ipilimumab + nivolumab is far superior to ipilimumab alone.
wrong.B: Pembrolizumab monotherapy achieves 26-33% intracranial ORR in melanoma brain metastases (KEYNOTE-001 subset). This is inferior to BRAF/MEK combination for V600E-mutated disease, though immunotherapy has a more durable response when it occurs.
wrong.D: Temozolomide has marginal activity in melanoma (<10% ORR) and is not used as a systemic therapy for brain metastases from melanoma. Dacarbazine (parent compound) was formerly used but has been replaced by targeted therapy and immunotherapy.

explanation.guideline: NCCN Melanoma CNS Metastases Guidelines 2024 — BRAF V600E-mutated melanoma: BRAF/MEK inhibition first-line for rapidly progressing/symptomatic brain mets; immunotherapy for asymptomatic stable disease
explanation.trial: Davies MA et al. Lancet Oncol 2017 (COMBI-MB): 125 pts BRAF V600E-mutated melanoma brain mets — dabrafenib+trametinib; Cohort A (V600E, naive): intracranial ORR 58%, median intracranial PFS 5.6 months
explanation.pearl: BRAF V600E melanoma brain mets: dabrafenib+trametinib achieves 58% intracranial ORR (COMBI-MB). Ipilimumab+nivolumab achieves 57-82% iORR (CheckMate 204; ABC trial). For rapidly progressing/symptomatic disease, BRAF/MEK provides faster response. Durable responses more common with immunotherapy.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with small cell lung cancer (SCLC) achieves complete response to platinum-etoposide chemotherapy for extensive-stage disease. KPS 90. What is the standard recommendation for prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI)?

options.A: PCI is not recommended for extensive-stage SCLC
options.B: PCI 25 Gy in 10 fractions is recommended to prevent CNS relapse
options.C: Quarterly brain MRI surveillance is recommended instead of PCI, based on Takahashi 2017 trial
options.D: WBRT 30 Gy in 10 fractions is the standard PCI regimen

explanation.correct: The Takahashi et al. (Lancet Oncol 2017) Japanese phase III trial randomised 224 extensive SCLC patients in remission to PCI vs MRI surveillance (every 3 months). OS was not significantly different (10.1 months PCI vs 11.6 months MRI surveillance, NS). PCI significantly impaired neurocognitive function (MMSE: 10% decline PCI vs 0% MRI), while the MRI surveillance arm received early treatment at CNS relapse. This trial challenged the prior Slotman 2007 trial that supported PCI for extensive SCLC. Current practice has evolved: many European guidelines recommend MRI surveillance instead of PCI, particularly in patients who can undergo close surveillance. However, practice varies internationally — the 2007 EORTC PCI trial (Slotman) is still cited by some guidelines.

wrong.A: PCI was historically recommended for extensive SCLC in remission based on the Slotman 2007 EORTC trial (reduced 1-yr brain mets 14.6% vs 40.4%, improved 1-yr OS 27.1% vs 13.3%). However, the Takahashi 2017 trial showing MRI surveillance equivalence has substantially changed practice.
wrong.B: 25 Gy in 10 fractions is the standard PCI dose for SCLC when it is administered (based on EORTC PCI dose-fractionation trial). However, given the Takahashi trial evidence, MRI surveillance is the preferred alternative to PCI for extensive SCLC in the current era.
wrong.D: 30 Gy in 10 fractions is the therapeutic WBRT dose for brain metastases, not the PCI dose. PCI dose is lower (25 Gy/10 fr or 20 Gy/5 fr) to minimise cognitive toxicity in patients treated prophylactically.

explanation.guideline: ESMO SCLC Guidelines 2021 — Extensive SCLC in remission: MRI surveillance preferred over PCI based on Takahashi 2017; ASTRO Brain Metastases 2022 acknowledges both options
explanation.trial: Takahashi T et al. Lancet Oncol 2017: 224 pts extensive SCLC in remission — PCI vs MRI surveillance; OS 10.1 vs 11.6 months (NS); MRI arm: lower neurocognitive impairment; similar OS with early salvage treatment at CNS relapse
explanation.pearl: Extensive SCLC in remission: Takahashi 2017 (Lancet Oncol) — MRI surveillance every 3 months = PCI for OS, with superior neurocognitive preservation. PCI remains an option (particularly in limited-stage SCLC and where MRI surveillance is not feasible), but MRI surveillance is preferred in extensive-stage disease.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with known RCC (clear cell) undergoes resection of a solitary 3.2 cm left parietal brain metastasis. Pathology confirms renal cell carcinoma metastasis. Post-operatively, systemic staging shows 2 small pulmonary nodules. What is the most appropriate adjuvant strategy for the surgical bed?

options.A: Observation alone — RCC is radioresistant so post-operative RT is ineffective
options.B: Resection cavity SRS (15-18 Gy), as RCC brain metastases are controlled by local high-dose radiotherapy
options.D: Immediate re-craniotomy for wider margins if <1 cm margin achieved

explanation.correct: Resection cavity SRS is the correct adjuvant strategy after resection of a single RCC brain metastasis. While RCC is considered relatively radioresistant to conventional fractionated radiotherapy (2 Gy/fraction), it responds to the high biologically effective doses delivered by SRS. Multiple series demonstrate 80-90% local control at 12 months for RCC brain metastases treated with SRS. Cavity SRS is preferred over WBRT based on N107C trial evidence (better cognitive preservation, equivalent local control). RCC brain metastases in particular benefit from SRS given the intrinsic radioresistance to WBRT fractionation.

wrong.A: The radioresistance of RCC applies to conventional fractionated radiotherapy (low dose per fraction, e.g., 2 Gy), not to high-dose per fraction SRS. RCC metastases respond well to SRS — local control rates are 80-92%. Observation alone would result in 46% local recurrence (Patchell 1998) without adjuvant treatment.
wrong.C: WBRT has reduced efficacy for RCC metastases due to the radioresistant histology at conventional fractionation. Given equivalent or superior outcomes with cavity SRS and better cognitive preservation, WBRT is not preferred for RCC brain metastases.
wrong.D: Brain tumour surgery aims for complete resection within safe margins; re-craniotomy for wider margins is not a practice in neurosurgery as it would cause significant neurological morbidity without established benefit. Brain metastasis management relies on local therapy (SRS) not margin expansion.

explanation.guideline: AANS/CNS Brain Metastases Guidelines 2022 — Post-surgical adjuvant: cavity SRS preferred; NCCN Kidney Cancer v2024 — CNS metastases section
explanation.trial: Mahadevan A et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2014: SRS for RCC brain metastases — local control 90.7% at 12 months; RCC responds to SRS despite conventional RT radioresistance; N107C (Brown Lancet Oncol 2017) establishes cavity SRS over WBRT
explanation.pearl: RCC radioresistance = resistance to CONVENTIONAL fractionated RT (2 Gy/fraction), NOT to SRS. SRS achieves 90%+ local control for RCC brain mets. After resection, cavity SRS is standard (not WBRT). Same principle applies to melanoma — SRS effective despite conventional RT resistance.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with KPS 50, multiple (>10) brain metastases, leptomeningeal carcinomatosis, and progressive systemic NSCLC refractory to 3 prior lines of therapy presents for palliative management of neurological symptoms. Which treatment approach is most appropriate?

options.B: Palliative whole-brain radiotherapy 20 Gy in 5 fractions
options.C: Aggressive surgical resection of the largest metastasis
options.D: Maximum intensity chemotherapy including temozolomide and bevacizumab

explanation.correct: A patient with KPS 50, extensive brain metastases (>10), leptomeningeal disease, and refractory systemic cancer represents RPA Class III (median OS 2.3 months). The appropriate approach is short-course palliative WBRT (20 Gy in 5 fractions), which provides rapid symptom palliation and intracranial disease control within a regimen completing treatment in 1 week, minimising burden on the patient and family. Dexamethasone is co-administered for symptom control. Goals of care should be explicitly discussed — comfort-focused care may be appropriate if the patient prioritises quality over quantity of life. WBRT in this setting is purely palliative.

wrong.A: SRS to >10 metastases with leptomeningeal carcinomatosis and KPS 50 is technically impractical (SRS planning and treatment for >10 lesions is appropriate for patients with good performance status and no LMD) and not aligned with the palliative intent of care.
wrong.C: Surgical resection is not appropriate for a patient with KPS 50, multiple brain metastases, and leptomeningeal disease. Surgery carries significant peri-operative risk and would not address the widespread CNS disease.
wrong.D: Temozolomide and bevacizumab combination has modest activity in brain metastases from NSCLC but is not a standard regimen and is inappropriate for a KPS 50 patient who is refractory to 3 prior therapy lines.

explanation.guideline: AANS/CNS Guidelines for Brain Metastases 2022 — RPA class III management: palliative WBRT or best supportive care; ESMO Brain Metastases Guidelines 2021
explanation.trial: Gaspar LE et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 1997 (RPA classification): Class III (KPS<70) — median OS 2.3 months with WBRT; palliative WBRT 20 Gy/5 fr equivalent to 30 Gy/10 fr in poor performance status patients (Borgelt 1980)
explanation.pearl: RPA Class III (KPS <70): median OS 2.3 months. Palliative WBRT 20 Gy/5 fr provides rapid symptom control in 1 week — appropriate for poor-PS patients. Aggressive local or systemic treatment not appropriate. Goals of care discussion essential.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old woman with triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) develops a single 2.8 cm right frontal brain metastasis. She has no other known metastatic disease. Performance status excellent (KPS 90). What is the correct surgical decision-making consideration regarding extent of resection?

options.A: En-bloc gross total resection is preferred over piecemeal resection to reduce leptomeningeal dissemination risk
options.B: Partial resection is appropriate for this size lesion as it will be followed by SRS
options.C: TNBC brain metastases always require piecemeal internal decompression before capsule dissection
options.D: Surgery is contraindicated for TNBC brain metastases — SRS alone is mandatory

explanation.correct: En-bloc (intact capsule) resection is strongly preferred over piecemeal internal debulking for brain metastases of any histology, but particularly for those with high leptomeningeal dissemination propensity. En-bloc resection maintains the tumour capsule intact, preventing seeding of viable tumour cells into the CSF compartment. Piecemeal resection of brain metastases is associated with 10-14% leptomeningeal carcinomatosis risk vs 5-6% with en-bloc technique. For TNBC brain metastases, the high tumour aggressiveness and leptomeningeal propensity make en-bloc technique especially important.

wrong.B: Partial resection is not appropriate for a resectable 2.8 cm lesion in a patient with excellent performance status. GTR followed by cavity SRS achieves superior local control compared to partial resection + SRS. Partial resection would still require adjuvant treatment but with inferior tumour control.
wrong.C: Piecemeal internal decompression is NOT the preferred technique for brain metastasis surgery. En-bloc resection with intact capsule is the standard to reduce LMD risk. There is no special indication for piecemeal resection in TNBC.
wrong.D: Surgery is not contraindicated for TNBC brain metastases. The indications for surgery (good PS, single accessible lesion, symptomatic, systemic disease controlled) apply equally to TNBC. In fact, surgical resection of a single brain metastasis in a patient with otherwise favourable features and possible oligometastatic disease is strongly indicated.

explanation.guideline: AANS/CNS Brain Metastases Guidelines 2022 — Surgical technique: en-bloc resection recommended to minimise LMD; EANO Brain Metastases Guidelines 2017
explanation.trial: Suki D et al. J Neurosurg 2016: En-bloc vs piecemeal resection of brain metastases — LMD rate 5.8% en-bloc vs 14.2% piecemeal (p<0.01); LMD dramatically worsens OS (median 4.1 months after diagnosis of LMD)
explanation.pearl: En-bloc (intact capsule) > piecemeal resection for brain metastases: LMD rate 5.8% vs 14.2%. LMD after brain met resection = catastrophic (median OS 4 months). En-bloc technique is especially important for cerebellar metastases and histologies prone to LMD (melanoma, breast, lung adenocarcinoma).

questions.stem: In the GPA (Graded Prognostic Assessment) scoring system for brain metastases, EGFR and ALK mutation status are incorporated as scoring components specifically for which primary tumour histology?

explanation.correct: The Diagnosis-Specific GPA (DS-GPA) for NSCLC was specifically modified by Sperduto et al. (JCO 2012, updated 2020) to include EGFR and ALK mutation status as scoring components, given that these molecular alterations dramatically improve prognosis. EGFR-mutated NSCLC with GPA 3.5-4.0 achieves median OS of 46.8 months — completely transforming the prognosis compared to EGFR/ALK-wild type NSCLC. This molecular inclusion in the GPA was driven by the availability of highly effective CNS-penetrant TKIs (osimertinib, alectinib).

wrong.A: The Breast DS-GPA (Breast-GPA) incorporates molecular subtype (HR+/HER2-, HER2+, TNBC) and KPS, but NOT EGFR/ALK (which are NSCLC-specific targets). HER2+ status is the dominant prognostic factor in breast cancer brain metastases.
wrong.B: The Melanoma DS-GPA incorporates BRAF V600E status and KPS, but not EGFR/ALK (which are not relevant to melanoma molecular profiling). BRAF status dramatically modifies melanoma prognosis.
wrong.D: The RCC DS-GPA does not incorporate EGFR or ALK. It uses KPS, extracranial metastases, and number of brain metastases. Molecular markers in RCC relevant to prognosis (e.g., IMDC risk factors) are not incorporated into the current RCC GPA.

explanation.guideline: Sperduto PW et al. J Clin Oncol 2012: Diagnosis-specific GPA for NSCLC includes EGFR/ALK status; updated 2020 to include EGFR exon-specific mutations
explanation.trial: Sperduto PW et al. J Clin Oncol 2020 (updated DS-GPA): NSCLC molecular subtype analysis — EGFR-mutated GPA 3.5-4: median OS 46.8 months; EGFR/ALK-negative GPA 0-1: median OS 3.4 months (13-fold difference in OS based on molecular status + GPA)
explanation.pearl: DS-GPA for NSCLC includes EGFR and ALK mutation status. EGFR+ GPA 3.5-4 = median OS 46.8 months — transforms prognosis. Melanoma DS-GPA includes BRAF status. Breast DS-GPA includes molecular subtype (HER2+/HR+/TNBC). Each histology has its own optimised prognostic variables.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with NSCLC (KRAS G12C mutated, PD-L1 50%, no EGFR/ALK) presents with a 4.5 cm right frontal brain metastasis with mass effect and 2 smaller asymptomatic metastases (0.9 cm each). Systemic disease is controlled on atezolizumab + bevacizumab + chemotherapy. What is the MOST appropriate combined local treatment strategy?

options.A: Surgery for the 4.5 cm lesion + SRS to both smaller lesions; defer WBRT
options.B: SRS to all 3 lesions simultaneously; avoid surgery
options.D: Surgery only for the 4.5 cm lesion; observe smaller lesions

explanation.correct: The correct approach for this patient combines surgery and SRS, avoiding WBRT. The 4.5 cm lesion with mass effect requires surgery — it is too large for safe single-fraction SRS (>40 mm exceeds RTOG 90-05 dosing range) and is causing neurological symptoms from mass effect. Surgical resection provides immediate decompression, tissue confirmation, and reduction of tumour burden. The two smaller lesions (0.9 cm each) are ideal SRS targets (≤20 mm, 24 Gy). WBRT is omitted because it provides no OS benefit while causing neurocognitive harm. Post-operatively, atezolizumab can be restarted after the perioperative period (steroid taper completed).

wrong.B: SRS alone to a 4.5 cm lesion with mass effect is inappropriate. Single-fraction SRS at this size carries high radionecrosis risk and does not address the acute mass effect. Fractionated SRS (5 fractions) could theoretically be considered but surgery is preferred for this size with mass effect.
wrong.C: WBRT alone is not the correct treatment for a patient with good performance status, controlled systemic disease, and a large symptomatic lesion requiring decompression. WBRT does not achieve rapid symptom relief and provides no OS benefit in this setting.
wrong.D: Observing the smaller lesions after surgery alone would leave 2 known metastases untreated, risking growth and eventual symptom development. SRS to both small lesions at the time of resection planning is the complete and correct approach.

explanation.guideline: AANS/CNS Brain Metastases Guidelines 2022 — Combined surgery + SRS approach for patients with mixed large/small metastases; ASTRO 2022 Brain Metastases Guidelines
explanation.trial: Patchell RA et al. NEJM 1990 established surgery for single large met; SRS alone: RTOG 90-05 limits for lesion size (>40 mm not suitable for SRS). Combined approaches evaluated in retrospective series (Flannery TJ et al. Int J Radiat Oncol 2008)
explanation.pearl: Brain mets >40 mm = not suitable for single-fraction SRS (RTOG 90-05). Symptomatic large mets with mass effect require surgery. Post-surgical cavity SRS + SRS to remaining small mets avoids WBRT (no OS benefit) while achieving comprehensive intracranial control.

questions.stem: The RTOG RPA (Recursive Partitioning Analysis) Class I criteria for brain metastases patients include which of the following?

options.A: KPS ≥70, age <65, controlled primary tumour, no extracranial metastases
options.B: KPS ≥70, age <65, active primary tumour, and >3 brain metastases
options.C: KPS <70, age any, controlled primary tumour

explanation.correct: RTOG RPA Class I requires ALL four criteria: KPS ≥70, age <65, controlled primary tumour, and absence of extracranial metastases. These patients have the best prognosis (median OS 7.1 months with WBRT in the original Gaspar 1997 analysis). Class III is defined by KPS <70 (any other features). Class II includes all patients not in Class I or III. The RPA was derived from three RTOG WBRT trials (7361, 7906, 8528) involving 1,200 patients.

wrong.B: Active primary tumour disqualifies from Class I (Class I requires controlled primary tumour). Additionally, >3 brain metastases would reduce GPA score but the RPA Class I specifically requires no extracranial metastases and a controlled primary tumour.
wrong.C: KPS <70 defines Class III automatically regardless of other factors. This answer describes Class III criteria, not Class I.
wrong.D: Age >70 disqualifies from Class I (Class I requires age <65). A 70-year-old with otherwise favourable features (controlled primary, no ECM, KPS ≥70) would be Class II.

explanation.guideline: RTOG RPA Classification (Gaspar et al. IJROBP 1997): Class I = KPS ≥70 + age <65 + controlled primary + no ECM; Class II = all others; Class III = KPS <70
explanation.trial: Gaspar LE et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 1997: 1,200 brain metastasis patients, 3 RTOG trials — RPA classification: Class I median OS 7.1 months; Class II 4.2 months; Class III 2.3 months. First validated prognostic system in brain metastases.
explanation.pearl: RPA Class I = KPS ≥70 + age <65 + controlled primary + no ECM → median OS 7.1 months. All 4 criteria must be met. RPA Class III = KPS <70 → median OS 2.3 months (sole criterion). Class II = everyone else → 4.2 months.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old woman with HER2-positive breast cancer undergoes surgical resection of a 3.5 cm left cerebellar brain metastasis. Histology confirms HER2+ breast cancer. Post-operative MRI shows complete resection. 6 weeks post-operatively, she develops nausea and ataxia; MRI shows a 4 cm enhancing lesion in the resection cavity. What is the most important diagnostic consideration to differentiate radiation effects from true local recurrence?

options.A: MR spectroscopy showing choline:NAA ratio >2.0 suggests recurrence over radionecrosis
options.B: CT imaging is sufficient to differentiate recurrence from radionecrosis
options.C: The 6-week time course is too short for radionecrosis — this must be true recurrence
options.D: The 4 cm enhancing lesion always represents recurrence after surgery

explanation.correct: Differentiating local recurrence from radiation-related changes (pseudoprogression, radionecrosis) after resection cavity SRS or WBRT is a major clinical challenge. MR spectroscopy is a useful adjunct: recurrent tumour shows elevated choline (cellular proliferation marker) and reduced NAA (neuronal integrity marker), with choline:NAA ratio >2.0 suggestive of recurrence. Radionecrosis shows reduced choline and NAA (tissue necrosis without active proliferation). Other advanced MRI techniques include perfusion imaging (high rCBV suggests recurrence; low rCBV suggests radionecrosis) and 18F-FET PET or 18F-FDOPA PET. Note: the 6-week time course is unusual for radionecrosis after SRS (typically 6-18 months) but pseudoprogression can occur earlier and treatment effect at the surgical site can be seen early.

wrong.B: CT imaging cannot reliably differentiate tumour recurrence from radionecrosis — both appear as enhancing lesions on CT. Advanced MRI techniques (spectroscopy, perfusion, susceptibility) or metabolic imaging (PET) are required.
wrong.C: While 6 weeks is early for classic radionecrosis after SRS (which typically occurs 6-18 months post-treatment), post-operative changes and early treatment effects can produce enhancement within weeks. The time course does not definitively establish recurrence vs treatment effect.
wrong.D: A 4 cm enhancing lesion after surgery and radiotherapy does NOT always represent recurrence. Post-operative changes, pseudoprogression (early treatment effect), and radionecrosis all produce enhancing lesions on MRI. Advanced imaging is required before concluding recurrence.

explanation.guideline: RANO (Response Assessment in Neuro-Oncology) brain metastases criteria; EANO Brain Metastases Guidelines 2017 — Distinguishing recurrence from treatment effect: MR spectroscopy, perfusion MRI, and FET-PET recommended
explanation.trial: Huang J et al. Clin Neurol Neurosurg 2011: MR spectroscopy in brain metastases after SRS — Cho:NAA >2.0 has 85% sensitivity, 75% specificity for recurrence vs radionecrosis; dynamic susceptibility contrast perfusion: rCBV >2.0 suggests recurrence
explanation.pearl: Recurrence vs radionecrosis after brain met treatment: MR spectroscopy (Cho:NAA >2.0 = recurrence), perfusion MRI (high rCBV = recurrence), 18F-FET/FDOPA PET (high uptake = recurrence). CT alone is insufficient. Time course: radionecrosis typically 6-18 months after SRS; pseudoprogression can be earlier (weeks).
